policy_statement: Support dam removals that reduce flood risk and improve aquatic habitat quality (DEC).
mechanism_description: The policy supports removing dams to restore river flow, which can reduce flood risk and improve aquatic habitat quality.
typology_evidence_quote: Support dam removals that reduce flood risk and improve aquatic habitat quality.
primary_causal_pathway: River infrastructure modification → restored natural flow/floodplain dynamics → ↓ flood hazard intensity/impacts → ↓ climate risk
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human/system change: Removal of existing dams and associated impoundments, often accompanied by channel/floodplain reconnection and sediment management.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Restores more natural river hydraulics and sediment transport, reduces upstream backwater effects and potential dam-failure risk, can increase conveyance and re-enable floodplain storage/dispersion in some settings; simultaneously improves longitudinal connectivity and habitat conditions (temperature, dissolved oxygen, migration pathways).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced damages and disruption from flooding under climate-driven increases in extreme precipitation and peak flows (primary), with co-benefits to ecosystem resilience and biodiversity.
dominant_pathway_test: Removing dams most strongly affects climate outcomes by reducing flood hazard impacts (lower exposure/vulnerability to high flows) rather than by directly changing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The main climate-relevant pathway is hazard/risk reduction—altering river infrastructure to reduce flood impacts as climate extremes intensify.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: While habitat restoration is important, the mechanism explicitly targets flood risk reduction; this is an exposure/vulnerability outcome rather than a primary emissions or energy-intensity outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some removals could marginally affect GHGs (e.g., reduced reservoir methane) or be framed chiefly as ecological restoration; however, across this mechanism as defined (“flood risk reduction and aquatic habitat restoration”), flood-risk reduction is the single biggest climate-relevant effect.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism entails dam removal/restored flow leading to reduced flood risk (even if magnitude varies by site), so Adaptation remains the primary category for the shared mechanism.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtype (e.g., Nature-Based Solutions co-benefit via habitat restoration) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Flood outcomes can be site-specific: in some geomorphic settings dam removal may not reduce downstream flood peaks and may temporarily increase sediment-related impacts; if a policy’s primary stated purpose is ecological restoration or water quality (with no material flood-risk objective), a Nature-Based Solutions primary could be more appropriate, but that would be outside this mechanism definition.
key_indicators: "Support dam removals"; "reduce flood risk"; "improve aquatic habitat quality"
co_benefits: Improved aquatic habitat quality (biodiversity/connectivity/water quality co-benefits)
instance_edge_case_notes: “Support” is somewhat ambiguous (could mean funding, permitting, or coordination), but the specified action is dam removal itself, so it is treated as an infrastructure investment/direct adaptation measure.
